front: Work: opens with the statement 'some things are up to us and some things are not up to us' and ends by quoting Cleanthes, Euripides, and Plato's Apology
Work: notes it makes more sense for a horse to say joyfully 'I am beautiful' than for its owner; recommends drinking cold water and spitting it out when thirsty
Concept: the defining faculty of mankind in Stoic thought, referring to man's power to choose how he reacts to impressions; contrasted with the 'aprohairetic'
Philosopher: James Stockdale was inspired by this thinker during his stint as a POW in Vietnam
Philosopher: identified two types of 'other-concern': aidos (modesty) and philanthropia (willingness to act on another's behalf)
Philosopher: outlined a 'doctrine of the three topics' consisting of the disciplines of desire, action, and assent
Philosopher: A.A. Long translated this thinker's Discourses as the book 'How to Be Free'
Work: a philosopher chooses calm over anger when his iron lamp is stolen; this conversational text discusses prohairesis and was the source for a shorter 'Handbook'
